channel.title: Nuno Nebeker
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2021, Nuno Nebeker; All rights reserved. Using Hugo and Color Your World.

item.title: The Organic and Social Nature of Software
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description: Programming has changed a lot through the years. Over the last few decades it seems like programmers have been competing over who uses the most arcane and inscrutable technology and process - see xkcd Real Programmers for reference. These days there’s a cultural shift to prove either we can be “orchestrators” or “supervisors” of generative Artificial Intelligence agents or we’re unnecessary and solo founders can “vibe code” their entire business. These extremes are predicated on the assumption that a computer program is a collection of lines of code.

item.title: Zine
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
I made a zine. That’s short for “magazine” and implies it’s a DIY project.

Art and hobbies are important. They let us use energy on things that matter to us and to the world, but aren’t jobs. The key is the pursuit of a process and enjoyment for their intrinsic value. I’ve considered myself an amateur photographer for about a decade and I keep looking for new ways to see things and share what I see. This is only one of them.

item.title: A11y Talks Porto - People Make Change
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
I’ve labelled myself an “accessibility advocate” for a few years now. I’m deeply involved in online blindness communities (OurBlind), I have training from Deque, I’ve worked with Siemens Portugal’s DivIn Employee Resource Group (link in Portuguese) on disability topics, and I’ve always strived to go one step further in building inclusive interfaces and systems to empower the people who use them (check out my older post The Thingy Didn’t Do the Thing!).

With all that, as a citizen of the Internet, I have precious few local connections with my local community of digital accessibility practitioners. When Patrícia Dias started bringing people together on LinkedIn for a meetup in Porto, I jumped at the chance.

item.title: An Inclusive City Nature Experience
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
In touch (at Flickr)

24C, mostly sunny, light wind on an August afternoon. In the Porto city center, the Serralves Foundation park offers a natural world enclave. By bringing nature into the built environment, it offers an unusual sensory experience: the Treetop Walk.

Contents

  • Contents
  • What is Serralves?
  • What’s a Treetop Walk and How Inclusive is it?
    • Physical Access
    • Sensory Experiences
    • Connection With The Rest of the Park
  • Inclusion and Connection

What is Serralves?

The Serralves Foundation seeks to share culture and art with a broad audience, and operates a contemporary art museum, a surrounding park and various other assets. It’s located in Porto, Portugal, near the city center.

item.title: Every Project Needs Accessibility
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
Today is Global Accessibility Awareness Day. If you don’t have the time or inclination to read my post, read this instead: W3C-WAI - Easy Checks – A First Review of Web Accessibility. If you can, read both!

When you build your shiny new website or web app remember:

  • If somebody with a motor disability can tab through it, so can you when your hands are busy.
  • If somebody with a visual impairment can use your website with a screen reader, it’ll be easy for you to use it with your voice assistant.
  • If somebody who’s hard of hearing can watch your video content with captions, so can you, if you’re in a noisy environment.
  • If you have text-only transcripts of all your content, a deafblind person can read them in Braille and you can find what you’re looking for, using a search engine.
  • If somebody with a cognitive disability won’t get tripped up by your interface, neither will you, when you’re tired or distracted.
  • If a person with low vision benefits from enough contrast to read, so do you, when out in the sun.

When you design for inclusivity, you’re not designing for people with disabilities: you’re designing for everyone.

item.title: How I Use Github as a CMS
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
There are many blogging and wiki options out there, and there are many factors that go into the decision to use one over another. I’ve used WordPress, in both its open source and hosted versions, and I’ve hand-crafted HTML and CSS when that’s what the project called for. I make no claims to know what the one-and-only blogging solution is, because I don’t think that exists.

Contents

  • Contents
  • My Stack
    • Writing in Markdown
    • Generating a Website with Hugo
    • Hosting With GitHub Pages
  • The CMS Part
  • Would I Recommend This?

My Stack

What I’m looking for is:

item.title: Alice & Bob Communicating as Humans
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
This post was originally published on LinkedIn. I’m making it available here in a bilingual format, with no tracking or metrics. If you like it, there’s nowhere to click, but do reach out and let me know.

Let’s Talk Communication!

Our good friends Alice and Bob need to communicate about something, so they use the coolest protocol out there, except they’re humans and they’re very different people.

item.title: The Thingy Didn't Do the Thing!
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description: I have some thoughts to share on user interface design. This is from the perspective of somebody who is involved in interface design, as a full-stack developer. The reader may not consider themselves to be involved in interface design, but if their work does things that interact with humans - that “interface,” if you will - they probably are. These thoughts have to do with taking responsibility for our designs, and I don’t want to make the reader uncomfortable with their own work or bring mine into play, so let’s consider a fictionalized scenario.

item.title: Owning My Words
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
I’ve had this little website for almost three years and only made a handful of posts. There are other platforms with websites and apps that don’t require me to create new Markdown files via CLI and rebuild and push a website. They also have much wider reach - immeasurably so.

As the sands of the internet continue to shift, one may want to ask the questions “are those advantages?” and “are they enough?” Regardless of one’s value judgments, it’s plainly evident that words we publish through somebody else’s platform are out of our control. One may be reminded of Scott Hansleman’s 2012 blog post when he advised you should:

item.title: AccessibleDocTool - creating accessible documents
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
I’ve gotten involved in a few different activities that warrant the creation of documents that are accessible to blind and visually impaired people. In general, that means careful font and visual style selection, for better visual readability, and proper use of semantics and structure, for screen reader use. It’s also a good idea to provide different file formats - different strokes for different folks, right?

That either sounds like a lot of work, or like a task that’s a perfect candidate for templates and automation. Through the magic of Pandoc and Markdown, I built a little project that, hopefully, makes the whole process easier: Accessibledoctool.

item.title: eul
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description: This week, I localized eul into Portuguese. It’s a system monitor for macOS that supports Big Sur, including some nice widgets.

item.title: Rectangle
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description:
Last week, I contributed a Portuguese (Portugal) localization to the Rectangle app.

It’s a window manager for macOS and I highly recommend it!

item.title: QR Codes
item.author: nuno@nebeker.tech (Nuno Nebeker)
item.description: QR codes for Nuno Nebeker links
